Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place because I'm juggling so much at 29 with limited resources. I tried to carve out about 32 hours a week, mostly in the evenings after work and on weekends. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other tasks like cooking or commuting. The routine wasn't pretty or Instagram-worthy, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on deadlines and what was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be incredibly consistent, studying at the same times every evening. Other weeks, especially when bills were tight or work got demanding, everything shifted around. I learned to be flexible because rigid schedules just weren't realistic for my situation.
Q3: Usually a looming deadline or the anxiety of falling behind would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post or new material and that would trigger me to check. My anxiety runs pretty high, so even when I couldn't fully engage, I'd log in just to see what I was missing and calm that nagging feeling in my chest.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never fully became routine for me—it was more of a tool I used when necessary. There were stretches where I'd log in daily to check discussions and watch videos, and that felt almost automatic. But then life would throw something at me and that habit would break. I'd have to rebuild the routine each time, which was exhausting.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me as an auditory learner—I watched over 200 minutes worth because hearing explanations really clicks for me. The discussion forums were also valuable because I could learn from other students' questions and perspectives. Those 13 posts I made weren't just for participation points; they genuinely helped me process the material by putting thoughts into words.
Q6: I often skipped or delayed the reading-heavy materials because staring at text on a screen with my limited resources—sometimes using my phone with spotty internet—was frustrating. Some of the supplementary PDFs just sat there untouched because I couldn't print them and reading on screen gave me headaches. I know I should have engaged more with those, but I had to prioritize what worked for my learning style.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to assessments or when it genuinely clarified something confusing. If I could listen to it while doing something else, even better. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers because at 29, I sometimes feel isolated from the typical student experience, and those connections made the learning feel more real.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford time-wise. Required ones got priority because my anxiety wouldn't let me rest until those were addressed. Sometimes I'd look at optional resources and feel guilty for not doing them, but I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. It's not that I didn't want to do them—I just had to make hard choices.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture audio and listen repeatedly on my phone during commutes or while doing chores. I took handwritten notes because that helps me remember things better, and I'd study from those for hours. I also used free YouTube videos and podcasts on related topics that wouldn't show up anywhere in the LMS.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd study intensively from my notes and downloaded materials, but my LMS activity would show nothing. I'd replay audio recordings I'd saved, talk through concepts out loud to myself, and work through problems on paper. My exam score of 84 didn't come from nowhere—it came from all that invisible work that the system never captured.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video lecture once, download the audio or take detailed notes, and then never open it again on the LMS. My task completion looks low at 51%, but that's because once I had what I needed, I didn't go back to click through things. I was studying the content; I just wasn't doing it in ways the system could track.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and engaging felt impossible. When money was tight or work demanded overtime, I'd fall behind and then the guilt would compound the anxiety. Sometimes I'd log in, see how much I'd missed, and feel so overwhelmed that I'd just close the laptop. Those were dark moments, but I kept pushing through.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were the biggest factor affecting my LMS use. I don't have the luxury of being a full-time student with family support—I work, I manage a household on limited resources, and I'm doing six online courses. Fatigue was real; some nights I'd log in with every intention of engaging but I'd just stare at the screen, too exhausted to absorb anything.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open videos or readings and my mind would be completely elsewhere, worrying about bills or work or just too tired to focus. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system, but I wasn't actually learning anything. I'd have to go back later and redo everything, which was frustrating and time-consuming.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. My anxiety would build up around them, making them feel bigger and scarier than they probably were. Sometimes I'd avoid starting because I was afraid of doing poorly, which is ironic because avoiding them guaranteed a worse outcome. The CBT sessions I did helped me recognize this pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my motivator. I'd often have bursts of intense LMS activity right before something was due. My 30% assignment completion rate doesn't tell the whole story—I'd prioritize the high-stakes submissions and let smaller things slide. Deadlines created structure when my life felt chaotic, but they also triggered my anxiety significantly.
Q17: Feedback had a huge impact on me emotionally and practically. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more; it validated that my unconventional study methods were working. Critical feedback sometimes sent me spiraling with anxiety, but my resilience kicked in and I'd use it to improve. I learned to seek feedback actively because it helped me focus my limited study time more effectively.
Q18: The course structure was a mixed bag for me. Having six courses simultaneously meant I had to constantly triage what got my attention. The modular structure helped me see what was coming, but sometimes the pacing didn't match my life's demands. I appreciated when instructors were clear about priorities because that helped me make strategic decisions about where to invest my energy.
Q19: Audio and video content made engagement easier for my learning style. What made it harder was when materials were only available in formats that required constant internet access or lots of reading on screen. Timed activities were stressful because my schedule was unpredictable. Flexible deadlines or extended windows for completion would have helped someone in my situation enormously.
Q20: I think high activity with low submissions could mean someone is struggling to understand the material despite trying hard, or they're dealing with anxiety about actually submitting work. It could also be someone like me who engages with content but then completes work offline or struggles with the final step of putting it all together. Activity doesn't equal output, especially when life is complicated.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My exam score was 84% and I got a Merit, but my LMS engagement was technically 'low.' I studied extensively offline, used downloaded materials, and processed information through listening and discussion rather than clicking through modules. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual learning effort.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline work, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They can't measure understanding or effort, just activity. For someone like me who learns by listening and reflecting, the logs probably make me look less engaged than I actually am.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely grappling with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking about concepts during my commute, or when I get excited about connecting ideas across different courses. It's participating in discussions because I'm curious, not just for points. Engagement is mental and emotional investment, which doesn't always translate to clicks and timestamps.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while working, or the late nights reviewing handwritten notes. It wouldn't capture the anxiety I pushed through to keep going, or the resilience it took to come back after falling behind. It wouldn't show that I'm a 29-year-old with limited resources who earned a Merit while managing six courses and a complicated life. That context matters.
